claims.evidence_text: Weeds that emerge with the wheat crop or early in the season are more competitive with wheat than weeds that emerge later in the season. Thus, winter annual weeds generally cause more yield reductions in winter wheat than do summer annual weeds.
claims.claim_summary: Weeds emerging with or early relative to the wheat crop are most competitive; early-season weed control is the critical period for winter annual weeds.

claims.evidence_text: a high population of bushy wallflower caused a 25 percent reduction in wheat yields from season long competition (Figure 3). Wheat yield reductions from bushy wallflower competition increased as treatment time was delayed.
claims.claim_summary: Season-long bushy wallflower competition (25 plants/sq ft) caused a 25% wheat yield reduction; losses rise as removal is delayed.

claims.evidence_text: Research at the Hays Experiment Station found that 100 cheat or downy brome plants per square yard, that emerged within one week of wheat emergence, reduced wheat yields over 35 percent (Figure 4). Cheat and downy brome at 100 plants per square yard, that didn’t emerge until 3 to 4 weeks after the wheat, only reduced wheat yields 12 and 6 percent, respectively.
claims.claim_summary: 100 cheat/downy brome plants per sq yd emerging within 1 week of wheat emergence reduced yield over 35%; emerging 3-4 weeks later reduced yield only 12% (cheat) and 6% (downy brome).

claims.evidence_text: Crop rotation is probably the best way to manage these weeds. Rotation to a summer crop helps break the life cycle of these weeds and allows the use of tillage or herbicides during the fall or spring to prevent seed production and deplete seed reserves in the soil.
claims.claim_summary: Crop rotation to a summer crop is the best cultural method to manage winter annual grasses (cheatgrass, jointed goatgrass, volunteer rye) in wheat.

claims.evidence_text: A delay in wheat planting date until several flushes of winter annual weeds have germinated and been destroyed with tillage or herbicides decreases the total number of weeds competing with the crop.
claims.claim_summary: Delaying wheat planting until several flushes of winter annual weeds have germinated and been destroyed reduces weed competition.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat generally is most tolerant to postemergence broadleaf control herbicides when the wheat is fully tillered, but prior to jointing. However, the proper wheat stage of growth for application depends on the specific herbicide. No herbicides are labelled for application when wheat is in the early boot to soft dough stage. Application during this time likely would result in sterility, poor grain fill, and reduced yields.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat is most tolerant to postemergence broadleaf herbicides when fully tillered but prior to jointing; no herbicides are labelled from early boot to soft dough stage.

claims.evidence_text: Postemergence herbicides generally are most effective when applied to small weeds that are actively growing. Winter annual broadleaf weeds are most susceptible when in the rosette stage of growth in the fall or early spring.
claims.claim_summary: Postemergence herbicides are most effective on small actively-growing weeds; winter annual broadleaf weeds are most susceptible at the rosette stage in fall or early spring.

claims.evidence_text: Kansas wheat is subject to more than 30 different diseases. Estimated statewide losses due to all diseases combined are approximately 20 percent per year. Individual fields may sustain losses of 50 percent or more.
claims.claim_summary: Kansas wheat is subject to >30 diseases; combined statewide losses average ~20% per year, with individual fields losing 50% or more.

claims.evidence_text: Resistance ratings are provided for wheat soilborne mosaic, wheat spindle streak mosaic, wheat streak mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, stem rust, Septoria leaf blotch, glume blotch, tan spot, powdery mildew, and Hessian fly. Resistance to the Russian wheat aphid and to the greenbug is available in some varieties. Some varieties, such as Karl 92, have partial resistance to scab. Some varieties also have moderate resistance to Stagonospora nodorum leaf blotch.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat variety resistance ratings cover soilborne mosaic, spindle streak mosaic, wheat streak mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, stem rust, Septoria leaf blotch, glume blotch, tan spot, powdery mildew, Hessian fly, Russian wheat aphid, and greenbug; Karl 92 has partial scab resistance.

claims.evidence_text: Resistant varieties are often the most effective, economical, and environmentally friendly method of disease control. ... Planting several different varieties with different strengths and weaknesses is a good disease management strategy. It reduces the risk that any particular disease will cause catastrophic losses.
claims.claim_summary: Resistant varieties are the most effective disease control method; planting several varieties with different resistances reduces risk of catastrophic disease losses.

claims.evidence_text: Crop rotation ... is very effective for controlling tan spot, dryland root rot, eyespot, Cephalosporium stripe, and take-all root rot. Crop rotation may reduce severity of scab, seedling blight, Septoria leaf blotch, Stagonospora leaf blotch, glume blotch, and sharp eyespot. One year of rotation or fallow is enough to break the cycle for most diseases.
claims.claim_summary: Crop rotation effectively controls tan spot, dryland root rot, eyespot, Cephalosporium stripe and take-all root rot; one year of rotation/fallow breaks the cycle for most wheat diseases.

claims.evidence_text: The take-all fungus can build up on barley, smooth brome, and weedy bromegrasses. The scab fungus builds up on corn and survives in surface corn residue. Therefore, avoid planting wheat after barley or brome, or into heavy corn residue.
claims.claim_summary: Avoid planting wheat after barley or brome (take-all carryover) or into heavy corn residue (scab carryover).

claims.evidence_text: Volunteer wheat can be a “green bridge” that allows pests to survive the period between wheat crops. Volunteer wheat can serve as a reservoir for wheat streak mosaic, High Plains mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, stem rust, and take-all root rot. It also harbors Hessian fly, Russian wheat aphids, greenbugs, Banks grass mites, and wheat curl mites.
claims.claim_summary: Volunteer wheat is a green bridge reservoir for wheat streak mosaic, High Plains mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, stem rust, take-all, and harbors Hessian fly, Russian wheat aphid, greenbug, Banks grass mite and wheat curl mite.

claims.evidence_text: volunteer wheat should be eliminated at least 1 /2 mile from wheat plantings. The objective is to break the green bridge before the new crop emerges. Therefore, volunteer should be eradicated at least 2 weeks before planting to ensure a thorough kill.
claims.claim_summary: Eradicate volunteer wheat at least 2 weeks before planting (and within 1/2 mile of plantings) to break the green bridge for pests/diseases.

claims.evidence_text: early planting is also a risk factor for several diseases including wheat streak mosaic, High Plains mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, sharp eyespot, common root rot, and take-all root rot. Planting after the Hessian fly-free date reduces the risk of these diseases.
claims.claim_summary: Early planting raises risk of wheat streak mosaic, High Plains mosaic, barley yellow dwarf, sharp eyespot, common root rot and take-all; planting after the Hessian fly-free date reduces risk.

claims.evidence_text: Seed treatments are excellent for control of seedborne diseases such as common bunt and loose smut. In order to keep our seed supply clean, seed treatments are highly recommended for all seed production fields. Seed treatments also can reduce seed rot and seedling blight. ... Certain seed treatments give partial control of barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, Septoria blotch, Stagonospora blotch, powdery mildew, take-all root rot, common root rot, or dryland foot rot.
claims.claim_summary: Seed treatments control seedborne common bunt and loose smut, reduce seed rot/seedling blight, and give partial control of barley yellow dwarf, leaf rust, Septoria, Stagonospora, powdery mildew, take-all, common root rot and dryland foot rot.

claims.evidence_text: Foliar fungicides are one of the few disease management tools that can be used in the spring. Fungicides can control leaf rust, tan spot, Septoria leaf blotch, Stagonospora nodorum leaf blotch, and powdery mildew. Degree of control ranges from poor to excellent, depending on the particular disease, fungicide, application timing, and rate. ... Fungicides are most likely to be economical in seed production fields or high yield potential fields.
claims.claim_summary: Spring foliar fungicides control leaf rust, tan spot, Septoria leaf blotch, Stagonospora nodorum leaf blotch and powdery mildew; most economical in seed production or high yield potential fields.

claims.evidence_text: Chloride deficiency has been linked to greater susceptibility to foliar diseases. Excess nitrogen can promote the development of powdery mildew. Liming of highly acid soils often triggers the activity of take-all root rot in continuous wheat.
claims.claim_summary: Chloride deficiency increases foliar disease susceptibility, excess nitrogen promotes powdery mildew, and liming acid soils triggers take-all root rot in continuous wheat.

claims.evidence_text: The best example is scab, which attacks only when rainy weather coincides with flowering.
claims.claim_summary: Scab (Fusarium head blight) attacks wheat only when rainy weather coincides with flowering (the disease-sensitive stage).

claims.evidence_text: Barley yellow dwarf Barley yellow dwarf virus ... Occasional losses of 30%, mostly in eastern and central Kansas. Early planting. High populations of aphid vectors in fall. Resistant varieties. Delayed planting. Seed treatments.
claims.claim_summary: Barley yellow dwarf virus causes occasional 30% losses; favored by early planting and high fall aphid vector populations; managed by resistant varieties, delayed planting and seed treatments.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat streak mosaic Wheat streak mosaic virus ... Can cause severe losses in western and central Kansas; rare in eastern Kansas. Hail at harvest causes early volunteer. Wet summer favors volunteer. Early planting. Planting near volunteer wheat. Warm fall favors wheat curl mite vector. Resistant varieties. Volunteer control. Delayed planting.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat streak mosaic virus (wheat curl mite vector) causes severe losses in western/central Kansas; favored by early planting, volunteer wheat and warm fall; managed by resistant varieties, volunteer control and delayed planting.

claims.evidence_text: Leaf rust Puccinia recondita f. sp. tritici Bright orange pustules on leaves. Important most years, especially eastern and central Kansas. Mild winter. Wet spring. Resistant varieties. Foliar fungicides. Seed treatments. Volunteer control.
claims.claim_summary: Leaf rust (Puccinia recondita f. sp. tritici) is important most years in eastern/central Kansas, favored by mild winter and wet spring; managed by resistant varieties, foliar fungicides, seed treatments and volunteer control.

claims.evidence_text: Scab (also called Fusarium head blight) Fusarium graminearum Group 2 ... Can cause severe losses in eastern and north-central Kansas; grain may contain mycotoxins. Rain and high humidity during flowering. Corn stalks on soil surface. Resistant varieties. Diversification of variety maturity. Crop rotation (except planting into corn residue).
claims.claim_summary: Scab/Fusarium head blight (Fusarium graminearum) causes severe losses with mycotoxins in eastern/north-central Kansas, favored by rain/high humidity during flowering and surface corn stalks; managed by resistant varieties, maturity diversification and crop rotation away from corn residue.

claims.evidence_text: Powdery mildew Blumeria graminis f.sp. tritici (also called Erysiphe graminis) Cottony white fungus on leaves, stems, and heads. ... High nitrogen fertilization. Cool temperatures. High humidity. Resistant varieties. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen. Foliar fungicides. Seed treatments.
claims.claim_summary: Powdery mildew (Blumeria graminis f.sp. tritici) is favored by high nitrogen, cool temperatures and high humidity; managed by resistant varieties, avoiding excess nitrogen, foliar fungicides and seed treatments.

claims.evidence_text: Tan spot Pyrenophora tritici repentis Symptoms appear in late March or early April. ... Important in continuous, minimum tillage wheat, especially in central and eastern Kansas. Continuous, minimum tillage wheat. Wet spring. Resistant varieties. Crop rotation. Wheat crop residue destruction. Foliar fungicides.
claims.claim_summary: Tan spot (Pyrenophora tritici-repentis) appears late March/early April, important in continuous minimum-tillage wheat; favored by continuous wheat and wet spring; managed by resistant varieties, rotation, residue destruction and foliar fungicides.

claims.evidence_text: Take-all root rot Gaeumannomyces graminis f.sp. tritici White heads; stunting; shiny black discoloration of stem base; black, rotten roots. ... Continuous wheat with minimum tillage. Early planting. Planting wheat after barley or brome . Good soil moisture, especially in fall. Volunteer wheat. Liming soils. Crop rotation (except barley and brome). Wheat crop residue destruction. Delayed planting. Volunteer control in continuous wheat. Seed treatments.
claims.claim_summary: Take-all root rot (Gaeumannomyces graminis f.sp. tritici) favored by continuous min-till wheat, early planting, planting after barley/brome and liming; managed by crop rotation (not barley/brome), residue destruction, delayed planting, volunteer control and seed treatments.

claims.evidence_text: Cephalosporium stripe Hymenula cerealis ... Continuous wheat with minimum tillage. Acid soils. Freezing and thawing of soil. Crop rotation. Wheat crop residue destruction. Delayed planting. Maintain pH above 5.5.
claims.claim_summary: Cephalosporium stripe (Hymenula cerealis) favored by continuous min-till wheat, acid soils and freeze-thaw; managed by crop rotation, residue destruction, delayed planting and maintaining pH above 5.5.

claims.evidence_text: Common bunt Tilletia caries or Tilletia laevis ... Seedborne on exterior of seed. Seedling infection favored by cool temperatures during germination. Seed treatments. Certified seed.
claims.claim_summary: Common bunt (Tilletia caries/laevis), seedborne and favored by cool germination temperatures, is managed by seed treatments and certified seed.

claims.evidence_text: Three to five beetles per square foot may justify treatment, depending upon seedling vigor, and stand density and extent of infested area.
claims.claim_summary: Flea beetle fall action threshold in wheat: 3 to 5 beetles per square foot may justify treatment.

claims.evidence_text: Scout prior to planting, three to five grasshoppers per square yard should be controlled–if possible prior to planting.
claims.claim_summary: Grasshopper action threshold in fall wheat: 3 to 5 grasshoppers per square yard should be controlled, preferably before planting.

claims.unit: worm per 3 square feet of row
claims.evidence_text: One worm per 3 square foot of row may be of economic concern.
claims.claim_summary: False wireworm action threshold in wheat: one worm per 3 square feet of row may be of economic concern.

claims.evidence_text: Consider delaying the date of planting where early-fall grub counts average four to five or more per square foot in the upper 6-inch soil layer.
claims.claim_summary: White grub threshold in wheat: delay planting where early-fall grub counts average 4 to 5 or more per square foot in the upper 6-inch soil layer.

claims.unit: greenbugs per foot of drill row
claims.evidence_text: Infestation is becoming serious when greenbugs average about 50 per foot on 3- to 6-inch plants.
claims.claim_summary: Greenbug fall action threshold in wheat: ~50 greenbugs per foot of drill row on 3- to 6-inch plants is becoming serious.

claims.evidence_text: Spraying may be necessary on stunted, poorly tillered wheat if six to 10 mites per plant are common.
claims.claim_summary: Winter grain mite action threshold in wheat: 6 to 10 mites per plant common on stunted, poorly tillered wheat may warrant spraying.

claims.evidence_text: Inspect daily in fields with 20 to 25 percent "windowpane" damage, Treat if signs of feeding continues to increase.
claims.claim_summary: Fall armyworm action threshold in wheat: inspect daily at 20-25% 'windowpane' damage and treat if feeding continues to increase.

claims.evidence_text: Pale Western Cutworm ... Infestations of two or more worms per square foot, are generally considered damaging.
claims.claim_summary: Pale western cutworm action threshold in wheat: infestations of 2 or more worms per square foot are considered damaging.

claims.evidence_text: Army Cutworm ... Infestations averaging four to five per foot of row may justify treatment where wheat is thin with little top growth. Vigorous stands may tolerate higher numbers.
claims.claim_summary: Army cutworm action threshold in wheat: 4 to 5 worms per foot of row may justify treatment where wheat is thin; vigorous stands tolerate more.

claims.evidence_text: Treatment is generally advisable when infestation levels reach 250 to 300 per row foot, where beneficial insects are relatively inactive.
claims.claim_summary: Greenbug spring action threshold in wheat: treatment advisable at 250 to 300 per row foot where beneficial insects are inactive.

claims.evidence_text: Bird Cherry-oat Aphid ... Control not generally recommended; however, at unusually high numbers (50+ per tiller) treatment might be worthwhile.
claims.claim_summary: Bird cherry-oat aphid spring action threshold in wheat: treatment may be worthwhile at unusually high numbers of 50+ per tiller.

claims.evidence_text: Russian WheatAphid ... Consider treatment when 10 to 20 percent of the tillers show signs of infestation where the area of infestation occupies a significant portion of the field.
claims.claim_summary: Russian wheat aphid spring action threshold in wheat: consider treatment when 10 to 20 percent of tillers show infestation over a significant field area.

claims.evidence_text: True Armyworm ... Need for treatment is probable when present at levels of five to eight per foot before the flag leaf has been destroyed.
claims.claim_summary: True armyworm spring action threshold in wheat: treatment probable at 5 to 8 worms per foot before flag leaf destruction.

claims.evidence_text: A change in production practices is indicated in fields where infestations equal or exceed 10 percent or more of stems infested, ... The major spring symptom is stem breakage at a point just slightly above any of the joints. Consult publication MF-1076, The Hessian Fly.
claims.claim_summary: Hessian fly: a change in production practices is indicated where infestations equal or exceed 10% of stems infested.

claims.evidence_text: Hessian Fly ... Fall injury consists of stunted tillers often with an unusually large broad green leaf. Symptoms usually more prominent from mid October into early November. Often worse in early fields. ... In most areas of the state, main brood usually before fly-free date, but an additional brood possible.
claims.claim_summary: Hessian fly fall injury (stunted tillers, broad green leaf) is most prominent mid-October into early November and worse in early-planted fields; main brood occurs before the fly-free date.

claims.evidence_text: Bird Cherry-oat Aphid Feeding by this insect rarely results in serious plant damage, but it is most important as a vector of barley yellow dwarf. Disease prevention through aphid control has not been historically feasible.
claims.claim_summary: Bird cherry-oat aphid rarely causes direct damage but is the key vector of barley yellow dwarf in wheat; disease prevention via aphid control is not historically feasible.
